title: Longest Common Subsequence
description:
## Longest Common Subsequence

Given two strings `text1` and `text2`, return the length of their longest common subsequence.

A subsequence is a sequence derived from a string by deleting some (or no) characters without changing the order of the remaining characters. A common subsequence is one that appears in both strings.

If there is no common subsequence, return 0.

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= text1.length, text2.length <= 1000`
- `text1` and `text2` consist of only lowercase English letters.

examples.explanation: The entire string is a common subsequence.

hiddenCases.explanation: LCS = b or m or j

starterCode.python:
def longest_common_subsequence(text1: str, text2: str) -> int:
    pass

solutions.label: 2D Dynamic Programming — O(m·n)
solutions.explanation: `dp[i][j]` stores the LCS length of `text1[:i]` and `text2[:j]`. If the current characters match, extend the LCS from the diagonal (`dp[i-1][j-1] + 1`). Otherwise, take the best of ignoring the current character of either string. Build up from base cases where one string is empty (LCS = 0).

solutions.label: Space-Optimized DP — O(m·n) time, O(n) space
solutions.explanation: Since each row only depends on the previous row, we can replace the full 2D table with two rolling arrays. After processing each row, swap `curr` into `prev`. This reduces space from O(m·n) to O(n) with no change in time complexity.

solutions.label: Space-Optimized DP — O(m·n) time, O(n) space
solutions.explanation: DP where dp[i][j] = LCS of text1[:i] and text2[:j]. Space-optimize by keeping only two rows.

hints: Build a 2D dp table where dp[i][j] = LCS length for the first i characters of text1 and first j characters of text2.
If text1[i-1] == text2[j-1], extend the LCS from the diagonal. Otherwise, take the max of excluding the current character from either string.
